item.title: CapsuleGAN - Improving GANs with CapsuleNET
item.description:
Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have shown remarkable success in generating realistic images, but they still suffer from some limitations such as mode collapse and instability during training. One approach to addressing these issues is to use Capsule Networks, a deep learning architecture that can better handle hierarchical representations and spatial relationships.

Capsule Networks were introduced by Hinton et al. in 2017 as an alternative to convolutional neural networks (CNNs). They are composed of capsules, which are groups of neurons that encode the presence and pose of an object in an image. Capsule Networks are designed to better handle hierarchical representations by modeling the parts of an object and their spatial relationships.

Our project implements the Capsule Network architecture in the discriminator of a GAN model and incorporates the Capsule Network margin loss into the objective function. Our results show that CapsuleGAN generates more diverse and realistic images than traditional GAN models. We also found that CapsuleGAN is more stable during training as evidenced by the lower variation in the generator and discriminator losses. We used the CIFAR-10 dataset to train and evaluate our model.

The Capsule Network architecture was particularly effective in capturing spatial relationships between objects, leading to more realistic and coherent images. Capsule Networks can better handle hierarchical representations and spatial relationships, which are important for generating high-quality images. Incorporating the margin loss of Capsule Networks can also improve the stability of GANs during training.

In conclusion, our project demonstrates the potential of Capsule Networks in enhancing the performance of GAN models and advancing the field of generative deep learning. Future research could explore further improvements by incorporating Capsule Networks into other types of GAN models or applying them to different domains beyond image generation.

item.title: Early Fault Detection of Turbo Engines using Time Series Analysis
item.description: Developing a method for predicting the remaining useful life (RUL) of a dynamic system is essential to deploy timely maintenance operations and extending the life of service. In this project, we propose an LSTM based Time Series Analysis method for early fault detection and alert system. As a first step, we will use the NASA Turbo Engines dataset for training our algorithm. The method will use historical data from 26 sensors to predict the future state (RUL) of the Turbo Engine. Further, we would like to extend this model into the real world predictive maintenance task. The project has the potential to improve the safety and reliability of any dynamic system performing under stress (e.g. aircraft operations) by providing early detection of faults.
